stem: A sequence is defined by \( a_0 = [[a0]] \) and \( a_{k+1} = [[r]]a_k + ([[d]]) \) for \( k \ge 0 \). What is \( a_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Apply \( a_{k+1} = [[r]]a_k+([[d]]) \) repeatedly starting from \( a_0=[[a0]] \); after [[n]] steps, \( a_{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the additive term \( d \) entirely, treating the recurrence as purely geometric.
This treats the recurrence as purely additive, ignoring the multiplicative factor \( r \).
This applies the recurrence only ONCE instead of \( n \) times.
This applies the recurrence one time too few before adding the last additive term.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A sequence satisfies \( a_0 = [[a0]] \) and \( a_{k+1} = [[r]]\,a_k \) for \( k \ge 0 \). What is \( a_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: A purely multiplicative recurrence gives a geometric sequence: \( a_n = a_0 r^n = [[a0]]\cdot[[r]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one power of \( r \), as if \( a_0 \) already counted as one application.
This treats the recurrence as additive instead of multiplicative.
This swaps the roles of the ratio and the index in the exponent.
This applies one extra multiplication by \( r \) beyond what is needed.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A sequence satisfies \( a_{k+1} = [[r]]\,a_k + [[d]] \) for all \( k \ge 0 \), and as \( k\to\infty \) the sequence converges to a limit \( L \) (regardless of \( a_0 \)). What is \( L \)?
solution: At the fixed point, \( L = [[r]]L + [[d]] \), so \( L(1-[[r]]) = [[d]] \) and \( L = \dfrac{[[d]]}{1-([[r]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( 1+r \) instead of \( 1-r \) when isolating \( L \).
This multiplies by \( (1-r) \) instead of dividing by it.
This divides by \( r \) alone instead of \( 1-r \).
This reports the additive term itself, ignoring the feedback from \( r \).
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.

stem: A sequence satisfies \( a_{k+1} = [[p]]\,a_k + [[q]]\,a_{k-1} \) for \( k \ge 1 \), with \( a_0 = [[a0]] \) and \( a_1 = [[a1]] \). What is \( a_5 \)?
solution: Applying the recurrence step by step: \( a_2=[[a2]] \), \( a_3=[[a3]] \), \( a_4=[[a4]] \), and \( a_5 = [[p]]\cdot[[a4]] + [[q]]\cdot[[a3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( a_4 \), one term too early.
This drops the \( q\,a_{k-1} \) contribution to the recurrence.
This swaps the roles of \( p \) and \( q \) in the final step.
This is really just \( a_2 \), stopping after only one application of the recurrence.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
